claims.text: After WWII, the U.S. and Soviet Union competed not only for Nazi gold and weapons but for Nazi scientists, recruiting over 1,600 (Operation Paperclip) and over 2,500 (Operation Osoaviakhim) respectively to fuel the Cold War scientific rivalry.

share of the nazi resources gold weapons
and oil were highly prized but no
resource was as valuable as the nazi
scientific community the united states
launched operation paperclip which
brought over 1600 nazi scientists
engineers and technicians to the u.s for
government employment the soviets had
operation osavea kim where over 2500
scientists were recruited by the soviet
union
abducted thank you this influx of talent
kicked off a scientific rivalry that
would last the next half century nuclear
technology was of course a priority for
weapons and for energy the most high
profile competition was th

claims.rationale: Operation Paperclip (U.S.) brought over 1,600 German scientists, engineers, and technicians to America between 1945 and 1959 — Wernher von Braun being the most famous. Operation Osoaviakhim (USSR, October 1946) forcibly relocated thousands of German specialists; the 2,500 figure is in the typical range cited. The framing of these recruitments as the trigger for the Cold War science race is also standard.

claims.text: The Soviet Kola Superdeep Borehole on the Kola Peninsula reached over 12 kilometers — about one third of the way through the Earth's crust — making it the deepest hole ever drilled, and it remains the deepest as of the time of recording.

who know

claims.rationale: The Kola Superdeep Borehole (SG-3) reached a final depth of 12,262 meters in 1989 and remains the deepest borehole ever drilled into the Earth's crust. The 'one third of the way through' framing is approximately correct for continental crust thickness (~35-40 km in that region).

claims.text: In 1989 a whistleblower claimed Soviet scientists drilling in Siberia at 14.4 kilometers broke through into a hollow cavern with temperatures over 1,100 degrees, and a microphone lowered into the hole recorded what sounded like millions of human souls screaming.

claims.rationale: The 'Well to Hell' story is a well-known hoax. No 14.4-km Siberian borehole exists in the Soviet drilling record (the deepest, Kola, reached 12.262 km on the Kola Peninsula in northwest Russia, not Siberia). 'Dr. Dimitri Azzacov' has no verifiable affiliation. The screaming-souls claim is the heart of an urban legend, not a documented incident. The host explicitly debunks it later in the video.

claims.text: After the recording, half the scientists quit immediately, a gas plume in the shape of a winged demon erupted from the hole spelling out 'I have conquered', and the KGB had medics inject the survivors with a memory-erasing sedative.

claims.rationale: Pure folklore embellishment of the original Trinity Broadcasting Network story. No KGB documentation or medic testimony exists outside the urban-legend retellings. The 'three words written in gas' detail was, per the host's own account later, added by the Norwegian prankster Olav Drønnesund. Memory-erasing drugs that work this cleanly do not exist.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the well-to-hell narrative with the demonic post-script.

claims.text: Coast-to-Coast AM host Art Bell received and broadcast in 1998 an email containing a 17-second audio file said to be the BBC-sourced recording from the Russian well to hell, and that broadcast is what cemented the legend in modern American paranormal radio.

claims.rationale: Art Bell did broadcast the well-to-hell audio on Coast to Coast AM in the late 1990s. This broadcast is widely credited (including in Snopes' canonical writeup) as the moment the recording became a fixture in American conspiracy/paranormal culture. The 'his uncle had a friend at the BBC' framing is the email's own claim and was never verified.

claims.text: The well-to-hell story originated in 1989 when the Trinity Broadcasting Network ran a 'Scientists Discover Hell' segment, sourced (per TBN) to a Finnish 'scientific journal' that turned out to be a small evangelical Lutheran magazine printing unverified reader submissions.

claims.rationale: Christian radio host Rich Buehler did track the story's chain of transmission in 1990 and traced it back through TBN to 'Ammennusastia' (a Finnish-language Pentecostal newsletter, not a scientific journal). The Buehler debunking is the canonical sourcing exposé and was reprinted by Snopes. The host's account matches it accurately.

dependencies.rationale: Establishes the original story had no scientific source — undermines the claim that real Soviet scientists experienced this.

claims.text: Most of the gory details of the well-to-hell story (the demon, the gas) were added as a deliberate prank by Norwegian schoolteacher Olav Drønnesund, who sent TBN a fake translation of an unrelated Norwegian newspaper article to test how gullible American media would be — and they ran it without checking.

evidence.quote:
rwegian
teacher named ogden rendaun heard the
original tbn story when he was visiting
california he was so amazed that
americans were gullible enough to
believe it he conducted an experiment he
got back to norway and found some boring
newspaper article and sent it to tbn but
he included a fake translation this is
how the whole to hell story picked up
the details about the demon it was a
prank made up by someone just to see if
tgn would fall for it and they did tgn
didn't fact check didn't do their own
translation they just saw a great story
and ran with it the media sure publishes
a lot of int

claims.rationale: The Drønnesund prank is part of the established debunking record for this legend. He has confirmed in interviews that he sent TBN a doctored translation as an experiment in American media credulity, and the demon/conqueror details first appear in his fabrication. The host's spelling 'Ogden Rendaun' garbles the actual name but the underlying account is correct.

dependencies.rationale: Identifies the demon/winged-figure details as a deliberate fabrication rather than reportage.

claims.text: The 17-second 'screaming souls' audio is not a recording from any borehole at all — it is a layered, looped sample taken from the soundtrack of the 1972 Mario Bava horror film 'Baron Blood'.

entional hoaxes don't they
if they get clicks they sure do anyway
the story has been resurfacing every few
years ever since sometimes the hole is
in alaska sometimes it's in norway
sometimes the demon is just a big bat
but that audio clip is always included
which adds a little extra eeriness to it
and no those aren't the screams of
millions of souls in hell it's actually
a layered and looped audio section from
a horror movie
no most people think it's from the 1972
be horror movie baron blood
[Applause]
as much as i love the whole to hell
story i think we can call this one
debunked these days there aren't too
many deep dril

claims.rationale: The 'Baron Blood' attribution is the standard skeptical identification of the audio's provenance. The clip has been spectrum-analyzed and matched to the film by multiple investigators. There's still some debate about which exact horror film it's from, but the host's hedge ('most people think') accurately reflects that.

dependencies.rationale: The Bell-broadcast audio that supposedly came from the BBC was actually a horror movie sample.
The screaming recording — the only 'physical' evidence for the story — turns out to be fictional.

claims.text: The actual Kola Superdeep Borehole produced real, scientifically valuable findings — including microscopic fossilized plankton and large reserves of mineralized water deep in the crust — but no evidence of demons, hell, or anything supernatural.

evidence.quote:
as of now the kola super deep
borehole in russia is still the deepest
at 12 kilometers cola superdeep has made
some interesting discoveries like
fossilized plankton and huge amounts of
mineralized water buried deep beneath
the surface but no demons no demons at
least not yet but if they keep drilling
who know

claims.rationale: Kola did unexpectedly recover microfossils of marine plankton at depths where they were not expected and discovered free water (hot brine) trapped in deep crystalline rock — both genuine surprises that became standard items in geophysics curricula. The 'no demons' part is trivially true.
